title: Chapter 1
content:
📖 Titus Chapter 1 (KJV)

📜 Chapter 1 Structure at a Glance

📖 Verse-by-Verse Breakdown

✦ SECTION 1 — Paul's Salutation (Verses 1–4)
"Paul, a servant of God, and an apostle of Jesus Christ, according to the faith of God's elect, and the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness; In hope of eternal life, which God, that cannot lie, promised before the world began; But hath in due times manifested his word through preaching, which is committed unto me according to the commandment of God our Saviour; To Titus, mine own son after the common faith: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ our Saviour."
— Titus 1:1–4 (KJV)

✦ SECTION 2 — Qualifications of Elders (Verses 5–9)
"For this cause left I thee in Crete, that thou shouldest set in order the things that are wanting, and ordain elders in every city, as I had appointed thee."
— Titus 1:5 (KJV)

Why Crete?
Crete was a large island known for moral corruption and disorder. Paul had left Titus there specifically to organize the churches and appoint qualified leadership.

📊 Qualifications of a Bishop/Elder — Titus 1:6–9

"For a bishop must be blameless, as the steward of God."
— Titus 1:7 (KJV)

Note: The word "bishop" (v.7) and "elder" (v.5) are used interchangeably here — referring to the same office of church overseer.

🔗 Comparison: Elder Qualifications in Titus vs. 1 Timothy

✦ SECTION 3 — Warning Against False Teachers (Verses 10–16)
"For there are many unruly and vain talkers and deceivers, specially they of the circumcision: Whose mouths must be stopped, who subvert whole houses, teaching things which they ought not, for filthy lucre's sake."
— Titus 1:10–11 (KJV)

📊 Profile of False Teachers in Titus 1

🔗 Key Cross-References for Titus 1

🕊️ Key Spiritual Lessons from Titus 1

"Holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught, that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort and to convince the gainsayers."
— Titus 1:9 (KJV)

title: Chapter 1
content:
📖 Titus Chapter 2 (KJV)

📜 Chapter 2 Structure at a Glance

📖 Verse-by-Verse Breakdown

✦ SECTION 1 — Instructions to Older Men (Verses 1–2)
"But speak thou the things which become sound doctrine: That the aged men be sober, grave, temperate, sound in faith, in charity, in patience."
— Titus 2:1–2 (KJV)

Note: Verse 1 opens with "But" — a direct contrast to the false teachers described in Chapter 1. Titus is to be the opposite of those deceivers.

Key Insight: The older man in the church is to model maturity in three areas — Faith (toward God), Charity (toward others), and Patience (under trial). These mirror the three great virtues of 1 Corinthians 13:13 — Faith, Hope, and Love.

✦ SECTION 2 — Instructions to Older Women (Verses 3–4a)
"The aged women likewise, that they be in behaviour as becometh holiness, not false accusers, not given to much wine, teachers of good things; That they may teach the young women to be sober."
— Titus 2:3–4a (KJV)

Key Insight: The word "false accusers" in the Greek is diabolos — the same root word for devil/slanderer. A gossiping, slanderous tongue is literally doing the devil's work.

✦ SECTION 3 — Instructions to Young Women (Verses 4b–5)
"To love their husbands, to love their children, To be discreet, chaste, keepers at home, good, obedient to their own husbands, that the word of God be not blasphemed."
— Titus 2:4b–5 (KJV)

"...that the word of God be not blasphemed."
— Titus 2:5b (KJV)
Key Insight: The ultimate reason for godly womanhood is not cultural but theological — so that the Word of God is honoured, not dishonoured before a watching world.

✦ SECTION 4 — Instructions to Young Men (Verses 6–8)
"Young men likewise exhort to be sober minded. In all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works: in doctrine shewing uncorruptness, gravity, sincerity, Sound speech, that cannot be condemned; that he that is of the contrary part may be ashamed, having no evil thing to say of you."
— Titus 2:6–8 (KJV)

Key Insight: For young men, the primary command is "sober minded" — appearing once here (v.6), but the concept echoes throughout. Young men are especially prone to passion, impulse, and pride. The antidote is sober-mindedness — disciplined thinking and self-mastery.
"In all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works."
— Titus 2:7 (KJV)

Titus himself is part of the instruction — he is not just to teach young men but to be a living model before them.

✦ SECTION 5 — Instructions to Servants (Verses 9–10)
"Exhort servants to be obedient unto their own masters, and to please them well in all things; not answering again; Not purloining, but shewing all good fidelity; that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things."
— Titus 2:9–10 (KJV)

"...that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things."
— Titus 2:10b (KJV)
Key Insight: The word "adorn" (kosmeo in Greek) means to decorate or beautify — the same root as our word cosmetics. A servant's faithful conduct makes the Gospel attractive and beautiful to the watching world. Every Christian's daily work is a display of the doctrine they profess.

📊 Summary of Instructions — Titus 2:1–10

✦ SECTION 6 — The Grace of God (Verses 11–14) ⭐ Heart of the Chapter
"For the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men, Teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly, in this present world; Looking for that blessed hope, and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ; Who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all iniquity, and purify unto himself a peculiar people, zealous of good works."
— Titus 2:11–14 (KJV)

📊 The Three Tenses of Salvation in Titus 2:11–13

📖 Breaking Down Verses 11–14

🔍 Three Dimensions of Godly Living — Titus 2:12

✦ SECTION 7 — Titus's Charge (Verse 15)
"These things speak, and exhort, and rebuke with all authority. Let no man despise thee."
— Titus 2:15 (KJV)

Key Insight: Titus is given three tools of ministry — speaking, exhorting, and rebuking. All three are necessary. A minister who only speaks but never rebukes allows error to grow. One who only rebukes but never exhorts discourages the flock.

🔗 Key Cross-References for Titus 2

🕊️ Key Spiritual Lessons from Titus 2

🔍 The Gospel in Titus 2:11–14 (Summary)

"For the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men, Teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly, in this present world."
— Titus 2:11–12 (KJV)

title: Philemon
content:
📖 The Book of Philemon — KJV Study

📌 Quick Facts

🗺️ Background & Context

title: Chapter 1
content:
📖 Philemon Chapter 1 (KJV)

📜 Structure at a Glance

📖 Verse-by-Verse Breakdown

✦ SECTION 1 — Greeting (Verses 1–3)
"Paul, a prisoner of Jesus Christ, and Timothy our brother, unto Philemon our dearly beloved, and fellowlabourer, And to our beloved Apphia, and Archippus our fellowsoldier, and to the church in thy house: Grace to you, and peace, from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ."
— Philemon 1:1–3 (KJV)

Key Observations:
Paul identifies himself as "a prisoner of Jesus Christ" — not ashamed of his chains, but anchored in Christ
Apphia is believed to be Philemon's wife
Archippus is believed to be his son (see Colossians 4:17)
The church met in Philemon's house — a common early Christian practice
Paul opens with his signature greeting: Grace and Peace

✦ SECTION 2 — Thanksgiving & Prayer (Verses 4–7)
"I thank my God, making mention of thee always in my prayers, Hearing of thy love and faith, which thou hast toward the Lord Jesus, and toward all saints; That the communication of thy faith may become effectual by the acknowledging of every good thing which is in you in Christ Jesus. For we have great joy and consolation in thy love, because the bowels of the saints are refreshed by thee, brother."
— Philemon 1:4–7 (KJV)

Key Observations:
Paul prays for Philemon consistently — setting a model of intercessory prayer
Philemon was known for his love and faith toward both Christ and fellow believers
"Bowels" in KJV language means the innermost affections — the deep seat of emotion
Paul is strategically building up Philemon before making his appeal — noting his character of refreshing others

✦ SECTION 3 — The Appeal for Onesimus (Verses 8–16)
"Wherefore, though I might be much bold in Christ to enjoin thee that which is convenient, Yet for love's sake I rather beseech thee, being such an one as Paul the aged, and now also a prisoner of Jesus Christ. I beseech thee for my son Onesimus, whom I have begotten in my bonds."
— Philemon 1:8–10 (KJV)

"Not now as a servant, but above a servant, a brother beloved, specially to me, but how much more unto thee, both in the flesh, and in the Lord?"
— Philemon 1:16 (KJV) ⭐ Key Verse

✦ SECTION 4 — Paul's Personal Pledge (Verses 17–21)
"If thou count me therefore a partner, receive him as myself. If he hath wronged thee, or oweth thee ought, put that on mine account; I Paul have written it with mine own hand, I will repay it."
— Philemon 1:17–19 (KJV)

Key Observations:
Paul says "receive him as myself" — a picture of substitution and advocacy
Paul personally guarantees any debt Onesimus owes — he picks up the debt of another. This is a beautiful type of Christ, who paid our debt before God
Paul then gently reminds Philemon: "thou owest unto me even thine own self" — Philemon himself came to faith through Paul
Verse 21 — Paul expresses confidence that Philemon will do even more than asked

✦ SECTION 5 — Closing (Verses 22–25)
"But withal prepare me also a lodging: for I trust that through your prayers I shall be given unto you. There salute thee Epaphras, my fellowprisoner in Christ Jesus; Marcus, Aristarchus, Demas, Lucas, my fellowlabourers. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. Amen."
— Philemon 1:22–25 (KJV)
Paul expresses hope to visit Philemon in person soon
He sends greetings from 5 companions: Epaphras, Marcus (Mark), Aristarchus, Demas, and Lucas (Luke)
Closes with a grace benediction — his standard letter ending

🔗 Key Cross-References

🕊️ Key Themes

🔍 Christ Typology in Philemon

One of the most beautiful aspects of this short letter is how it pictures the Gospel:

"And all things are of God, who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ, and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation."
— 2 Corinthians 5:18 (KJV)
